summary: In February 1870, Mississippi minister and Civil War chaplain Hiram Revels became the first African American to serve in the U.S. Senate, filling the seat once held by Jefferson Davis. He resigned within a year to become the founding president of Alcorn University, the nation's first state-supported land-grant college for Black Americans.
locationLabel: Alcorn State University (founded as Alcorn University), Lorman, Mississippi

claims.object: Hiram Revels was sworn into the U.S. Senate on February 25, 1870, becoming the first African American to serve in the United States Senate, seated in the Mississippi seat once held by Jefferson Davis.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. Senate: Hiram Revels, First African American Senator

claims.object: Revels resigned his Senate seat in 1871 to become the first president of Alcorn University (now Alcorn State University), the first state-supported land-grant college for Black Americans in the United States.
claims.citationLabel: Mississippi Encyclopedia: Alcorn State University

historicalContext: Revels is anchored at Alcorn rather than the U.S. Capitol because his most enduring legacy was institutional: he left the Senate after roughly a year to build the college he considered his life's real work.

summary: Joseph Hayne Rainey, born enslaved in Georgetown, South Carolina, escaped to Bermuda during the Civil War and returned to become the first African American to serve in the U.S. House of Representatives, seated December 12, 1870. In 1874 he became the first Black lawmaker to preside over the House from the Speaker's chair.
locationLabel: Joseph H. Rainey House, 909 Prince Street, Georgetown, South Carolina

claims.object: Rainey took his seat in the U.S. House of Representatives on December 12, 1870, becoming the first African American to serve in the House.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. House of Representatives: Representative Joseph Rainey of South Carolina

claims.object: After the Civil War, Rainey settled in this Prince Street house, which served as his home and district headquarters throughout his congressional career; it was designated a National Historic Landmark in 1984.
claims.citationLabel: Clio: Joseph H. Rainey House

historicalContext: Rainey later became, on April 29, 1874, the first Black member to preside over the House chamber, overseeing debate as Speaker pro tempore.

summary: Born into slavery near Farmville, Virginia, Blanche K. Bruce rose to become, in 1875, the first African American to serve a full six-year term in the U.S. Senate, representing Mississippi. He later served as Register of the U.S. Treasury and Recorder of Deeds for the District of Columbia.
locationLabel: Blanche Kelso Bruce birthplace marker, intersection of Route 360 and Route 623, Green Bay, Prince Edward County, Virginia

claims.object: The Mississippi legislature elected Bruce to the U.S. Senate in February 1874; he served from March 1875 to March 1881, the first Black senator to complete a full term.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. Senate: Blanche K. Bruce, A Featured Biography

claims.object: Bruce was born into slavery on March 1, 1841, near Farmville in Prince Edward County, Virginia; a Virginia Department of Historic Resources marker near Green Bay commemorates his birthplace.
claims.citationLabel: Historical Marker Database: Blanche Kelso Bruce

historicalContext: Bruce is anchored at his Virginia birthplace marker rather than his Senate seat or his later Washington, D.C. career, since it is the site most directly and durably commemorated in his honor.

summary: Pinckney Benton Stewart Pinchback, a Union Army officer and Louisiana state senator, served as acting governor of Louisiana from December 9, 1872 to January 13, 1873, becoming the first Black governor of a U.S. state. He later helped found Southern University and was buried in New Orleans.
locationLabel: Pinchback family plot, Metairie Cemetery, New Orleans, Louisiana

claims.object: Pinchback served as acting governor of Louisiana from December 9, 1872 to January 13, 1873 after the impeachment of Governor Henry Warmoth, becoming the first Black governor of a U.S. state.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. Senate: Reconstruction Louisiana and the Case of P.B.S. Pinchback

claims.object: Pinchback is buried with his mother in the family plot at Metairie Cemetery in New Orleans.
claims.citationLabel: 64 Parishes: P.B.S. Pinchback

historicalContext: Before his brief governorship, Pinchback had already become president pro tempore of the Louisiana State Senate and acting lieutenant governor after Oscar Dunn's death in 1871.

summary: Elected associate justice of the South Carolina Supreme Court in February 1870, Jonathan Jasper Wright became the first Black state supreme court justice in U.S. history and the first Black man elected to any state or federal appellate judgeship. He practiced law in Charleston until his death in 1885.
locationLabel: Charleston, South Carolina, where Wright practiced law and died

claims.object: The South Carolina General Assembly elected Wright as an associate justice of the state Supreme Court on February 1, 1870, making him the first Black state supreme court justice in the United States.
claims.citationLabel: South Carolina Encyclopedia: Jonathan Jasper Wright

claims.object: After resigning from the court in 1877 amid a Reconstruction-era impeachment campaign, Wright returned to private law practice in Charleston, where he died in 1885.
claims.citationLabel: Brennan Center for Justice: Jonathan Jasper Wright, America's First Black State Supreme Court Justice

historicalContext: Wright served on the court for nearly seven years and wrote roughly ninety opinions; he remained the only Black state supreme court justice in the country for almost a century after Reconstruction ended.

summary: Born enslaved in Beaufort, South Carolina, Robert Smalls commandeered the Confederate steamer CSS Planter in 1862 and delivered it to the Union blockade, freeing himself and his crew. He went on to serve five terms in Congress and became the first Black captain of a U.S. vessel.
locationLabel: Robert Smalls House, 511 Prince Street, Beaufort, South Carolina

claims.object: On May 13, 1862, Smalls piloted the Confederate transport CSS Planter out of Charleston harbor and surrendered it to the Union Navy, becoming the first African American captain of a vessel in U.S. service.
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Robert Smalls

claims.object: This house at 511 Prince Street, where Smalls had been enslaved as a child, was purchased by him after the war and became his home; it was designated a National Historic Landmark in 1974.
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: The Robert Smalls House

historicalContext: Smalls represented Beaufort in Congress for five terms between 1875 and 1887 and rose to major general in the South Carolina militia, one of the era's most decorated Black public figures.

summary: John Mercer Langston, the first Black lawyer admitted to the Ohio bar, became the founding dean of Howard University's law school and, in 1885, the first president of what is now Virginia State University. In 1890 he was seated as Virginia's first Black member of Congress.
locationLabel: Virginia State University (founded as Virginia Normal and Collegiate Institute), Ettrick, Virginia, near Petersburg

claims.object: In January 1886, Langston became the first president of the Virginia Normal and Collegiate Institute, now Virginia State University, serving until December 1887.
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopedia Virginia: John Mercer Langston

claims.object: After successfully contesting a disputed 1888 election result, Langston was sworn into the 51st Congress on September 23, 1890, becoming Virginia's first Black member of the U.S. House of Representatives.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. House of Representatives: John Mercer Langston

historicalContext: Until 1992, Langston remained the only Black Virginian ever elected to Congress; he was also the great-uncle of poet Langston Hughes.

summary: On July 3, 1844, Macon Bolling Allen was admitted to the Maine bar by examination, becoming the first African American licensed to practice law in the United States. He later co-founded the first known Black law firm in the country in Charleston, South Carolina.
locationLabel: Portland, Maine, where Allen was admitted to the bar in 1844

claims.object: Allen was licensed to practice law in Maine on July 3, 1844, after passing a qualifying examination, becoming the nation's first African American lawyer.
claims.citationLabel: Federal Defender Services: Macon Bolling Allen, First Black Lawyer and Judge

claims.object: In 1847, Allen passed a qualifying exam to become Justice of the Peace in Middlesex County, Massachusetts, becoming the second African American to hold a judicial position in the United States.
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: Macon Bolling Allen (1816-1894)

historicalContext: Allen later moved to Charleston, South Carolina, where his firm, Whipper, Elliott, and Allen, is recognized as the first known African American law firm in the country.

summary: Charlotte E. Ray graduated from Howard University's law school in 1872, applying under the initials "C.E. Ray" to disguise her gender, and became the first Black woman lawyer in the United States and the first woman admitted to practice before the D.C. Supreme Court.
locationLabel: Howard University, Washington, D.C., where Ray earned her law degree in 1872

claims.object: Ray graduated from Howard University School of Law in 1872 and was admitted to the District of Columbia bar, becoming the first Black woman lawyer in the United States.
claims.citationLabel: History.com: Charlotte E. Ray's Brief But Historic Career

claims.object: Ray became the first woman admitted to practice before the Supreme Court of the District of Columbia.
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: Charlotte E. Ray (1850-1911)

historicalContext: Discrimination against both her race and her gender made it impossible to sustain a law practice, and Ray later moved to New York to teach; her bar admission nonetheless became a legal precedent cited by women in other states.

summary: Mifflin Wistar Gibbs, a California gold-rush merchant and abolitionist newspaper founder, moved to Little Rock, Arkansas after the Civil War and in 1873 became the first elected African American municipal judge in the United States, serving as Little Rock's police judge.
locationLabel: Little Rock, Arkansas, where Gibbs served as police judge and later died

claims.object: Gibbs was elected Little Rock police judge, serving from November 1874 to April 1875, becoming the first elected African American municipal judge in the United States.
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopedia of Arkansas: Mifflin Wistar Gibbs

claims.object: In San Francisco in 1855, Gibbs co-founded Mirror of the Times, the first Black newspaper published west of the Mississippi River.
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopedia of Arkansas: Mifflin Wistar Gibbs

historicalContext: Gibbs later served as U.S. consul to Madagascar and lived out his life in Little Rock, where he died in 1915.

summary: Born enslaved in Louisiana, John Roy Lynch became, at age 24, the first African American speaker of the Mississippi House of Representatives in 1872, then represented Mississippi in Congress for three terms, one of the most prominent Black Reconstruction-era officeholders.
locationLabel: Old Mississippi State Capitol Museum, 100 State Street, Jackson, Mississippi

claims.object: In 1872, at age 24, Lynch became the first African American Speaker of the Mississippi House of Representatives, presiding in this building, then the state capitol.
claims.citationLabel: Mississippi Department of Archives and History: John R. Lynch Exhibit at Old Capitol

claims.object: Lynch served in the U.S. House of Representatives across the 43rd, 44th, 45th, and 47th Congresses, and in 1884 became temporary chairman of the Republican National Convention, the first Black man to deliver its keynote address.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. House of Representatives: Representative John Roy Lynch of Mississippi

historicalContext: The Old Capitol, now a state history museum, maintains a permanent exhibit honoring Lynch's tenure as speaker.

summary: Alexander Thomas Augusta, commissioned an Army surgeon in 1863, became the highest-ranking Black officer of the Civil War and the first African American to head a U.S. hospital and to teach at a U.S. medical college. In 1890 he became the first Black officer buried at Arlington National Cemetery.
locationLabel: Arlington National Cemetery, Section 1, Arlington, Virginia

claims.object: Commissioned a major in April 1863 as head surgeon of the 7th U.S. Colored Infantry, Augusta became the U.S. Army's first Black surgeon and, by 1865, its highest-ranking Black officer as a brevet lieutenant colonel.
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: Alexander T. Augusta (1825-1890)

claims.object: Following his death in 1890, Augusta was buried with full military honors in Section 1 of Arlington National Cemetery, a section previously reserved for white officers, making him the first Black officer buried there.
claims.citationLabel: Self-Guided Tour: Black History of Arlington National Cemetery

historicalContext: Augusta also became the first Black professor of medicine in the United States, teaching at the newly founded Howard University Medical School after the war.

summary: Abolitionist, physician, and writer Martin Delany was commissioned a major in February 1865, becoming the first Black field officer in the U.S. Army. He recruited formerly enslaved men into United States Colored Troops regiments in the South Carolina Lowcountry.
locationLabel: Massies Creek Cemetery, Cedarville, Ohio, near Delany's Wilberforce home

claims.object: In February 1865, Delany was commissioned a major, becoming the first Black field officer in the United States Army, after proposing to President Lincoln a corps of Black soldiers led by Black officers.
claims.citationLabel: South Carolina Historical Society: Martin Delany Becomes the First Black Major in the U.S. Army

claims.object: Delany died in Wilberforce, Ohio, on January 24, 1885, and is buried in a family plot at Massies Creek Cemetery, about three miles from his home.
claims.citationLabel: Historical Marker Database: Martin Robison Delany

historicalContext: Delany was among the era's leading proponents of Black nationalism and emigration before the war, then served the Union cause directly as its highest-ranking Black officer.

summary: Sergeant William Harvey Carney of the 54th Massachusetts Volunteer Infantry retrieved and carried the regimental colors during the July 1863 assault on Fort Wagner despite severe wounds. His 1900 Medal of Honor recognized the earliest action of any Black Medal of Honor recipient.
locationLabel: Oak Grove Cemetery, 185 Parker Street, New Bedford, Massachusetts

claims.object: During the July 18, 1863 assault on Fort Wagner, Carney carried the U.S. flag forward after the color bearer fell; his Medal of Honor, awarded May 23, 1900, recognized the earliest battlefield action of any African American Medal of Honor recipient.
claims.citationLabel: Congressional Medal of Honor Society: William H. Carney, The First Medal of Honor Action by an African American

claims.object: Carney died in 1908 and is buried in the family plot at Oak Grove Cemetery in New Bedford, Massachusetts, where his headstone bears an image of the Medal of Honor.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. Army: Meet Sgt. William Carney, Medal of Honor Recipient

historicalContext: Carney later became one of the first documented Black postal mail carriers in New Bedford, Massachusetts.

summary: Carl B. Stokes was elected mayor of Cleveland in November 1967 and took office January 1, 1968, becoming the first African American elected mayor of a major U.S. city. His administration pursued police reform and community investment amid the era's urban unrest.
locationLabel: Cleveland City Hall, 601 Lakeside Avenue, Cleveland, Ohio

claims.object: Stokes was elected mayor of Cleveland on November 7, 1967, and took office January 1, 1968, becoming the first Black mayor of one of the ten largest U.S. cities.
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Carl B. Stokes, Ground-Breaking Cleveland Mayor

claims.object: Stokes defeated Seth Taft, grandson of former President William Howard Taft, with 50.5 percent of the vote.
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopedia of Cleveland History: Carl B. Stokes

historicalContext: Stokes took office the same day as Richard Hatcher of Gary, Indiana, though Stokes was inaugurated first.

summary: Richard Gordon Hatcher won election as mayor of Gary, Indiana in November 1967, becoming, alongside Cleveland's Carl Stokes, one of the first two African Americans elected mayor of a U.S. city with more than 100,000 people. He served twenty years, from 1968 to 1988.
locationLabel: Gary, Indiana, where Hatcher served twenty years as mayor

claims.object: Hatcher won Gary's mayoral election on November 7, 1967, becoming, with Carl Stokes of Cleveland, one of the first African Americans elected mayor of a U.S. city with a population over 100,000.
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: Richard G. Hatcher (1933-2019)

claims.object: Hatcher served as mayor of Gary for twenty years, from 1968 to 1988, after overcoming a primary marked by voter intimidation and vote-rigging attempts against his candidacy.
claims.citationLabel: NBC News: Richard Hatcher of Gary, Indiana, one of 1st Black mayors of a major U.S. city

historicalContext: Hatcher's win and Stokes's win came in the same November 1967 elections, marking the first wave of Black mayors elected in major American cities.

summary: Coleman Alexander Young took office as Detroit's first Black mayor in January 1974 and served five terms through 1994, disbanding a controversial police unit and pursuing police integration during a pivotal era in the city's history.
locationLabel: Coleman A. Young Municipal Center (Detroit City Hall), 2 Woodward Avenue, Detroit, Michigan

claims.object: Young took office as Detroit's first African American mayor in January 1974, going on to serve five consecutive terms until 1994.
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopaedia Britannica: Coleman Young

claims.object: Detroit's city government headquarters at 2 Woodward Avenue was renamed the Coleman A. Young Municipal Center in his honor.
claims.citationLabel: City of Detroit: Coleman A. Young Municipal Center

historicalContext: Young's platform centered on police reform; as mayor, he disbanded the controversial STRESS police unit and expanded integration within the department.

summary: Tom Bradley, a former Los Angeles police lieutenant and city councilman, defeated incumbent Sam Yorty on May 29, 1973, to become the first Black mayor of Los Angeles. He served an unprecedented five terms, twenty years, until 1993.
locationLabel: Los Angeles City Hall, 200 North Spring Street, Los Angeles, California

claims.object: Bradley defeated incumbent mayor Sam Yorty on May 29, 1973, becoming the first Black mayor of Los Angeles and one of the first Black mayors of a major U.S. city.
claims.citationLabel: California African American Museum: Tom Bradley Elected Los Angeles' First Black Mayor

claims.object: Bradley served twenty years as mayor of Los Angeles, from 1973 to 1993, the longest tenure in the city's history at the time.
claims.citationLabel: PBS SoCal: Tom Bradley Elected L.A. Mayor, 1st Black Mayor of a Major U.S. City

historicalContext: Bradley rose to lieutenant in the LAPD, the highest rank a Black officer had achieved at the time, before turning to elected office.

summary: Harold Washington won a multiracial coalition campaign to become Chicago's first Black mayor, inaugurated April 29, 1983, after a voter registration drive added over 127,000 African Americans to the rolls. He served until his death in office in 1987.
locationLabel: Chicago City Hall, 121 North LaSalle Street, Chicago, Illinois

claims.object: Washington was inaugurated at Navy Pier on April 29, 1983, becoming Chicago's first Black mayor after winning the general election with 52 percent of the vote.
claims.citationLabel: Chicago Public Library: Mayor Harold Washington Biography

claims.object: A voter registration drive organized by community groups, Black churches, and businesses added more than 127,000 African Americans to Chicago's voting rolls ahead of Washington's 1983 win.
claims.citationLabel: WTTW Chicago: Achieving the Dream, Harold Washington

historicalContext: Washington's multiracial coalition and progressive agenda are widely cited as an early influence on Barack Obama's political career in Chicago.

summary: David N. Dinkins, a former Manhattan borough president, was inaugurated January 1, 1990 as the 106th Mayor of New York City, becoming the first African American to hold the office. He implemented the Safe Streets, Safe City initiative during his single term.
locationLabel: New York City Hall, City Hall Park, Manhattan, New York

claims.object: Dinkins was inaugurated January 1, 1990 as the 106th Mayor of the City of New York, the first African American to hold the office.
claims.citationLabel: NYC Municipal Archives: David N. Dinkins, 106th Mayor of the City of New York

claims.object: Dinkins implemented the Safe Streets, Safe City criminal justice plan, under which the city's homicide rate declined substantially by the end of his term.
claims.citationLabel: NPR: David Dinkins, New York City's First Black Mayor, Dies at 93

historicalContext: Before becoming mayor, Dinkins served in the New York State Assembly and as Manhattan borough president.

summary: W. Wilson Goode Sr., a former city administrator who grew up in poverty in rural North Carolina, was inaugurated as Philadelphia's first Black mayor in January 1984 after defeating Frank Rizzo in the Democratic primary. He served two terms, from 1984 to 1992.
locationLabel: Philadelphia City Hall, 1500 Market Street area, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania

claims.object: Goode was inaugurated as Philadelphia's first African American mayor in January 1984, after defeating former mayor Frank Rizzo in the 1983 Democratic primary.
claims.citationLabel: Philadelphia Encyclopedia: W. Wilson Goode, Philadelphia's First African American Mayor

claims.object: Goode served two terms as mayor, from 1984 to 1992, a tenure significantly shaped by the 1985 MOVE police confrontation and house bombing.
claims.citationLabel: UPI Archives: W. Wilson Goode Calling His Inauguration as Philadelphia's First

historicalContext: Goode's election capped decades of civil rights organizing and demographic change in Philadelphia city politics.

summary: Willie L. Brown Jr. served as the first African American Speaker of the California State Assembly from 1980 to 1995, a record fourteen-year tenure, before winning election as San Francisco's first Black mayor, serving from 1996 to 2004.
locationLabel: San Francisco City Hall, 1 Dr. Carlton B. Goodlett Place, San Francisco, California

claims.object: Brown was elected mayor of San Francisco in 1995, taking office in 1996 as the city's first African American mayor, and served until 2004.
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopaedia Britannica: Willie Brown

claims.object: Brown served as Speaker of the California State Assembly from 1980 to 1995, the first African American to hold the post and its longest-serving speaker.
claims.citationLabel: California Museum: Willie L. Brown, Jr.

historicalContext: Brown's fourteen years as Assembly speaker made him one of the most powerful Black elected officials in the country before he turned to City Hall.

summary: Kenneth A. Gibson, a former city engineer, defeated two-term incumbent Hugh Addonizio in 1970 to become the first African American mayor of a major northeastern U.S. city, leading Newark, New Jersey for sixteen years until 1986.
locationLabel: Newark City Hall, 920 Broad Street, Newark, New Jersey

claims.object: Gibson defeated incumbent Hugh Addonizio in a 1970 runoff, becoming the first African American to serve as mayor of a major city in the northeastern United States.
claims.citationLabel: African American Intellectual History Society: Police Power and the Election of Newark's First Black Mayor

claims.object: In 1976, Gibson became the first African American president of the United States Conference of Mayors.
claims.citationLabel: Democracy Now!: Kenneth Gibson, Newark's First Black Mayor, Dies

historicalContext: Gibson served sixteen years as mayor of Newark, from 1970 to 1986, guiding the city through a period of significant fiscal and social strain.

summary: Maynard Holbrook Jackson Jr. was sworn in January 7, 1974, as Atlanta's first Black mayor and the first Black mayor of any major city in the American South. He served three non-consecutive terms and pioneered minority business inclusion programs.
locationLabel: Atlanta City Hall, 55 Trinity Avenue SW, Atlanta, Georgia

claims.object: Jackson was sworn in on January 7, 1974, as Atlanta's first Black mayor and the first Black mayor of any major city in the American South.
claims.citationLabel: New Georgia Encyclopedia: Maynard Jackson

claims.object: Jackson served as Atlanta's mayor for three terms, from 1974 to 1982 and again from 1990 to 1994, the second-longest tenure in the city's history.
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopaedia Britannica: Maynard Jackson

historicalContext: Jackson's administration required that Black-owned firms receive a substantial share of contracts for the new Hartsfield-Jackson airport terminal, a landmark minority-inclusion program.

summary: Ernest Nathan "Dutch" Morial took office May 2, 1978 as the first African American mayor of New Orleans, capping a career that also included being the city's first Black assistant U.S. attorney and the first Black Louisiana state legislator since Reconstruction.
locationLabel: New Orleans, Louisiana, where Morial served as the city's first Black mayor

claims.object: Morial took office as the first African American mayor of New Orleans on May 2, 1978, after winning 95 percent of the Black vote and 20 percent of the white vote.
claims.citationLabel: NOLA.com: Ernest 'Dutch' Morial Becomes City's First Black Mayor

claims.object: Before becoming mayor, Morial was the first Black graduate of LSU's law school, the first Black assistant U.S. attorney in Louisiana, and the first Black Louisiana state legislator since Reconstruction.
claims.citationLabel: Washington Post: Ernest Morial, 1st Black Mayor of New Orleans, Dies at 60

historicalContext: Morial was married to civil rights activist Sybil Haydel Morial, and their son Marc Morial later served as New Orleans mayor from 1994 to 2002.

summary: Sharon Pratt Dixon was sworn in January 2, 1991 as mayor of Washington, D.C., becoming the first African American woman to lead a major American city. She served a single term, from 1991 to 1995, as the district's first mayor born in the city since 1861.
locationLabel: John A. Wilson Building, 1350 Pennsylvania Avenue NW, Washington, D.C.

claims.object: Pratt was sworn in as mayor of the District of Columbia on January 2, 1991, becoming the first African American woman mayor of a large American city.
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: Sharon Pratt Dixon Kelly (1944- )

claims.object: Pratt was also the first mayor born in the District of Columbia to hold the office since Richard Wallach took office in 1861.
claims.citationLabel: Wikipedia: Sharon Pratt

historicalContext: Pratt served one term as mayor, from 1991 to 1995, governing from the John A. Wilson Building, seat of the District's government.

summary: Karen Bass, a former physician assistant and community organizer, was elected the first woman mayor of Los Angeles in 2022. She had earlier become, in 2008, the first Black woman in U.S. history to serve as speaker of a state legislative body, leading the California State Assembly.
locationLabel: Los Angeles City Hall, 200 North Spring Street, Los Angeles, California

claims.object: Bass was elected mayor of Los Angeles in 2022, becoming the city's first woman mayor and its second Black mayor after Tom Bradley.
claims.citationLabel: Wikipedia: Karen Bass

claims.object: In 2008, Bass was elected the 67th Speaker of the California State Assembly, becoming the first African American woman in U.S. history to serve as speaker of any state legislative body.
claims.citationLabel: The HistoryMakers: The Honorable Karen Bass's Biography

historicalContext: Bass anchors this record at Los Angeles City Hall, the site of her mayoral inauguration, though her first legislative-leadership milestone occurred in Sacramento.

summary: Oscar Stanton De Priest, a Chicago alderman and real estate businessman, won a special 1928 election to Congress, becoming the first African American elected to Congress outside the South and the first Black member of Congress in the twentieth century.
locationLabel: United States Capitol, Washington, D.C.

claims.object: De Priest won election to the U.S. House of Representatives from Illinois's 1st district in 1928, becoming the first Black member of Congress elected from a northern state and the first of the twentieth century.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. House of Representatives: The Birth and Career of Representative Oscar S. De Priest

claims.object: De Priest served in the U.S. House from 1929 to 1935, representing Illinois's 1st congressional district based in Chicago.
claims.citationLabel: Avoice Digital Library: Oscar Stanton De Priest

historicalContext: De Priest was the first Black member of Congress since George White of North Carolina left office in 1901, ending a 28-year absence of Black representation in Washington.

summary: Shirley Anita Chisholm won election to Congress from Brooklyn's 12th district in November 1968, becoming the first Black woman elected to the U.S. Congress. In 1972 she became the first Black candidate for a major party's presidential nomination.
locationLabel: United States Capitol, Washington, D.C.

claims.object: Chisholm won election in November 1968 to a newly created congressional district centered in Bedford-Stuyvesant, Brooklyn, becoming the first Black woman elected to the United States Congress.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. House of Representatives: The First African American Woman Elected to Congress

claims.object: In 1972, Chisholm became the first Black candidate for a major party's presidential nomination and the first woman to seek the Democratic Party's nomination.
claims.citationLabel: National Archives: Shirley Chisholm

historicalContext: Chisholm represented her Brooklyn district for seven terms, from 1969 to 1983, and was a founding member of the Congressional Black Caucus in 1971.

summary: Edward W. Brooke III, Massachusetts's attorney general since 1963, won election to the U.S. Senate in 1966, becoming the first African American senator elected by popular vote and the first Black senator since Reconstruction. He served two terms, from 1967 to 1979.
locationLabel: United States Capitol, Washington, D.C.

claims.object: Brooke defeated former Governor Endicott Peabody in 1966 and was seated in the Senate on January 3, 1967, becoming the first African American elected to the U.S. Senate by popular vote.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. Senate: Edward Brooke of Massachusetts, The Bridge Builder

claims.object: Brooke was elected Massachusetts attorney general in 1962, becoming the first African American elected attorney general of any U.S. state.
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopaedia Britannica: Edward Brooke

historicalContext: In the Senate, Brooke co-authored the Fair Housing Act of 1968 and became the first Black senator ever re-elected, in 1972.

summary: Barbara Jordan, the first Black woman elected to the Texas Senate since Reconstruction, was elected to the U.S. House in 1972, becoming the first woman descended from enslaved Americans elected to Congress. In 1976 she became the first Black keynote speaker at a major-party convention.
locationLabel: United States Capitol, Washington, D.C.

claims.object: Jordan was elected to the U.S. House of Representatives from Texas in 1972, becoming, with Andrew Young of Georgia, one of the first two Black members of Congress from the former Confederacy since 1901.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. House of Representatives: Barbara Charline Jordan

claims.object: On July 12, 1976, Jordan delivered the keynote address at the Democratic National Convention, the first African American to keynote a major party's national nominating convention.
claims.citationLabel: National Archives: The Keynote Speaker, Congresswoman Barbara Jordan

historicalContext: Jordan gained further national attention for her televised opening statement during the 1974 House Judiciary Committee hearings on the impeachment of Richard Nixon.

summary: Andrew Young, a former aide to Martin Luther King Jr., was elected to Congress from Georgia in 1972, the first Black congressman from the state since Reconstruction. In 1977 he became the first African American U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations.
locationLabel: United States Capitol, Washington, D.C.

claims.object: Young was elected to the U.S. House of Representatives from Georgia in 1972, becoming the first Black congressman from Georgia since Reconstruction.
claims.citationLabel: Today in Georgia History: Andrew Young

claims.object: In 1977, President Carter appointed Young U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations, the first African American to hold the post.
claims.citationLabel: National Archives: Andrew Young, A Giant Among Us

historicalContext: Young later served as mayor of Atlanta from 1982 to 1990, continuing a career that spanned the civil rights movement, Congress, diplomacy, and city government.

summary: L. Douglas Wilder, grandson of enslaved Virginians, was sworn in January 13, 1990 as governor of Virginia, becoming the first African American ever elected governor of a U.S. state. He had earlier become Virginia's first Black lieutenant governor.
locationLabel: Virginia State Capitol, Richmond, Virginia

claims.object: Wilder was sworn in as governor of Virginia on January 13, 1990, by former U.S. Supreme Court Justice Lewis F. Powell Jr., becoming the first African American ever elected governor of a U.S. state.
claims.citationLabel: History.com: Douglas Wilder of Virginia Becomes the Nation's First Elected Black Governor

claims.object: Wilder was elected in November 1989 by less than half a percentage point over Republican Marshall Coleman, one of the closest gubernatorial elections in Virginia history.
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopedia Virginia: L. Douglas Wilder

historicalContext: Wilder had previously served as Virginia's first Black lieutenant governor, elected in 1985, before his historic 1989 gubernatorial win.

summary: Carol Moseley Braun, a former Illinois state legislator and Cook County recorder of deeds, was elected to the U.S. Senate in 1992, becoming the first African American woman to serve in the Senate and only the second Black senator since Reconstruction.
locationLabel: United States Capitol, Washington, D.C.

claims.object: Moseley Braun was elected to the U.S. Senate from Illinois in 1992 and served from January 3, 1993 to January 3, 1999, the first African American woman to serve in the Senate.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. Senate: Carol Moseley Braun, A Featured Biography

claims.object: Moseley Braun was the first woman elected to the U.S. Senate from Illinois and only the second African American senator since Reconstruction.
claims.citationLabel: Biographical Directory of the U.S. Congress: Carol Moseley Braun

historicalContext: After leaving the Senate, Moseley Braun served as U.S. Ambassador to New Zealand and Samoa from 1999 to 2001.

summary: Deval Patrick, a former assistant U.S. attorney general for civil rights under President Clinton, took office January 4, 2007, as the first African American governor of Massachusetts and the second Black elected governor in U.S. history, after Douglas Wilder.
locationLabel: Massachusetts State House, 24 Beacon Street, Boston, Massachusetts

claims.object: Patrick took office January 4, 2007, as the first African American governor of Massachusetts and the second Black governor ever elected in the United States, after Douglas Wilder of Virginia.
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopaedia Britannica: Deval Patrick

claims.object: Patrick served two terms as governor, from 2007 to 2015, and had previously served as U.S. assistant attorney general for civil rights from 1994 to 1997.
claims.citationLabel: National Governors Association: Deval Patrick

historicalContext: Patrick's election followed the state's first Democratic governor since Michael Dukakis left office in 1991.

summary: David A. Paterson, New York's first Black lieutenant governor, was sworn in as governor on March 17, 2008, after Eliot Spitzer's resignation, becoming the first African American governor of New York and the fourth Black governor in U.S. history.
locationLabel: New York State Capitol, Albany, New York

claims.object: Paterson was sworn in as the 55th governor of New York on March 17, 2008, following Governor Eliot Spitzer's resignation, becoming the first African American governor of New York.
claims.citationLabel: Empire State Plaza: New York Leaders, Governor David A. Paterson

claims.object: Paterson was elected New York's first African American lieutenant governor in November 2006 before ascending to the governorship in 2008.
claims.citationLabel: National Governors Association: David A. Paterson

historicalContext: Paterson was also the first legally blind person to serve as governor of a U.S. state.

summary: Augustus "Gus" Hawkins, first elected to the California State Assembly in 1934, won election to Congress in 1962, becoming California's first Black congressman and the first Black member of Congress from any state west of the Mississippi River.
locationLabel: United States Capitol, Washington, D.C.

claims.object: Hawkins was elected to the U.S. House of Representatives from California in November 1962 and took office January 3, 1963, becoming the first Black member of Congress elected from any western state.
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: Augustus Hawkins (1907-2007)

claims.object: Hawkins had earlier been elected to the California State Assembly in 1934, defeating longtime incumbent Frederick M. Roberts, and served there for nearly three decades before moving to Congress.
claims.citationLabel: California Legislative Black Caucus Policy Institute: Hon. Augustus F. Hawkins

historicalContext: Hawkins later co-authored the Humphrey-Hawkins Full Employment Act of 1978, one of the signature legislative achievements of his congressional career.

summary: William Levi Dawson represented a Chicago congressional district for more than 27 years and, in 1949, became the first African American to chair a standing committee of the U.S. House of Representatives, leading the Committee on Expenditures in the Executive Departments.
locationLabel: United States Capitol, Washington, D.C.

claims.object: In 1949, Dawson became chair of the House Committee on Expenditures in the Executive Departments (later Government Operations), the first African American to chair a standing committee of Congress.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. House of Representatives: William Levi Dawson

claims.object: Dawson represented a Chicago district in Congress for more than 27 years, from 1943 until his death in office in 1970, holding the committee gavel for ten non-consecutive terms.
claims.citationLabel: Avoice Digital Library: William Levi Dawson

historicalContext: Dawson's committee chairmanship predated Adam Clayton Powell Jr.'s better-known 1961 chairmanship of the Education and Labor Committee by more than a decade.

summary: Adam Clayton Powell Jr. represented Harlem in Congress from 1945 to 1971 and, in 1961, became chairman of the House Education and Labor Committee, then the most powerful position held by an African American in Congress, shepherding major Great Society legislation.
locationLabel: United States Capitol, Washington, D.C.

claims.object: In 1961, Powell became chairman of the House Committee on Education and Labor, then the most powerful position held by an African American in Congress to that date.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. House of Representatives: Representative Adam Clayton Powell, Jr., of New York

claims.object: As committee chairman from 1961 to 1967, Powell's panel approved more than fifty measures establishing federal minimum-wage increases, school lunch programs, student loans, and aid to schools and libraries.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. House of Representatives: The Special Election of Adam Clayton Powell, Jr.

historicalContext: Powell represented New York's Harlem-based district for thirteen terms before the House Democratic Caucus stripped his chairmanship in January 1967 amid a contempt case and ethics disputes.

summary: Mervyn Dymally, a Trinidad-born California state senator, was elected the state's first Black lieutenant governor in 1974, serving from 1975 to 1979, when he cast the tie-breaking vote enacting California's first major gay rights legislation.
locationLabel: California State Capitol, Sacramento, California

claims.object: Dymally was elected California's first Black lieutenant governor in 1974, serving from 1975 to 1979.
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopedia.com: Mervyn M. Dymally

claims.object: As lieutenant governor, Dymally cast the tie-breaking vote in 1975 that enacted California's first major gay rights legislation.
claims.citationLabel: California State University, Dominguez Hills: Dymally Institute History

historicalContext: Dymally and George L. Brown of Colorado, elected the same year, were the first two African Americans elected lieutenant governor since Oscar Dunn's Reconstruction-era tenure in Louisiana.

summary: Robert C. Weaver was appointed by President Lyndon Johnson to lead the newly created Department of Housing and Urban Development on January 13, 1966, becoming the first African American to serve in a presidential Cabinet. HUD's headquarters building is named for him.
locationLabel: Robert C. Weaver Federal Building (HUD Headquarters), 451 7th Street SW, Washington, D.C.

claims.object: President Johnson appointed Weaver secretary of the newly created Department of Housing and Urban Development on January 13, 1966; the Senate confirmed him four days later, making him the first African American member of a U.S. Cabinet.
claims.citationLabel: Miller Center: Robert C. Weaver (1966-1969)

claims.object: HUD's headquarters building at 451 7th Street SW was renamed the Robert C. Weaver Federal Building in his honor.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. General Services Administration: Robert C. Weaver Federal Building, Washington, DC

historicalContext: As HUD secretary, Weaver championed the Fair Housing Act of 1968, which banned housing discrimination nationwide.

summary: Patricia Roberts Harris, daughter of a Pullman car waiter, was appointed Secretary of Housing and Urban Development by President Carter in 1977, becoming the first African American woman in a U.S. presidential Cabinet; she later also led the Department of Health and Human Services.
locationLabel: Robert C. Weaver Federal Building (HUD Headquarters), 451 7th Street SW, Washington, D.C.

claims.object: Harris was appointed Secretary of Housing and Urban Development by President Carter in 1977, becoming the first African American woman to serve in a U.S. presidential Cabinet.
claims.citationLabel: Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture: A Higher Standard, Patricia Roberts Harris

claims.object: Harris later became the first African American woman appointed to two different Cabinet posts, leading Health and Human Services from 1979 to 1981.
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopaedia Britannica: Patricia Roberts Harris

historicalContext: Harris served in the same HUD headquarters building later named for her Cabinet predecessor Robert C. Weaver, the first Black Cabinet secretary.

summary: Thurgood Marshall, the NAACP Legal Defense Fund attorney who argued Brown v. Board of Education, was sworn in October 2, 1967, as the first African American justice of the U.S. Supreme Court, serving 24 years and consistently opposing racial and gender discrimination.
locationLabel: Supreme Court of the United States, 1 First Street NE, Washington, D.C.

claims.object: Marshall was sworn in as Associate Justice of the U.S. Supreme Court on October 2, 1967, becoming the first African American to serve on the Court.
claims.citationLabel: History.com: Thurgood Marshall Sworn In as First Black Supreme Court Justice

claims.object: Before joining the Court, Marshall won 29 of the 32 cases he argued before it as an attorney, including Brown v. Board of Education of Topeka in 1954.
claims.citationLabel: National Archives Foundation: Justice Thurgood Marshall, First African American Supreme Court Justice

historicalContext: Marshall served on the Supreme Court for 24 years, until 1991, consistently opposing the death penalty and defending civil rights and affirmative action.

summary: Constance Baker Motley, an NAACP Legal Defense Fund attorney who won nine of ten civil rights cases she argued before the Supreme Court, was confirmed to the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York in 1966, becoming the first Black woman federal judge.
locationLabel: New York, New York, seat of the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York

claims.object: Motley was confirmed by the Senate on August 30, 1966, to the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York, becoming the first African American woman to serve as a federal judge.
claims.citationLabel: United States Courts: Constance Baker Motley, Judiciary's Unsung Rights Hero

claims.object: As an NAACP Legal Defense Fund attorney, Motley became the first Black woman to argue a case before the U.S. Supreme Court, winning nine of the ten civil rights cases she argued there.
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Constance Baker Motley

historicalContext: Motley later served as Chief Judge of the Southern District of New York from 1982 to 1986, after facing a seven-month confirmation delay led by Senator James Eastland.

summary: Jane Matilda Bolin, the first Black woman to graduate from Yale Law School, was appointed by Mayor Fiorello La Guardia at the 1939 New York World's Fair to the city's Domestic Relations Court, becoming the first Black woman judge in the United States.
locationLabel: Flushing Meadows Corona Park, site of the 1939 New York World's Fair, Queens, New York

claims.object: On July 22, 1939, at the New York World's Fair, Mayor Fiorello La Guardia appointed 31-year-old Jane Bolin a judge of the New York City Domestic Relations Court, making her the first Black woman judge in the United States.
claims.citationLabel: Historical Society of the New York Courts: Hon. Jane M. Bolin, Judging Across Decades

claims.object: Bolin was also the first Black woman to graduate from Yale Law School and the first to join the New York City Bar Association and the New York City Law Department.
claims.citationLabel: Biography.com: Jane Bolin

historicalContext: Bolin served on the bench for forty years, through three renewed appointments, remaining the only Black woman judge in the country for two decades.

summary: William Henry Hastie, appointed by President Roosevelt to the District Court of the Virgin Islands in 1937, became the first Black federal judge. In 1949 President Truman named him to the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit, seated in Philadelphia, the first Black federal appellate judge.
locationLabel: Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, seat of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit

claims.object: President Truman gave Hastie a recess appointment to the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit on October 21, 1949, and the Senate confirmed him in 1950, making him the first African American federal appellate judge.
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: William Henry Hastie (1904-1976)

claims.object: In 1937, President Roosevelt had appointed Hastie to the District Court of the Virgin Islands, making him the first African American to serve as a federal judge of any kind.
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: William Henry Hastie (1904-1976)

historicalContext: Hastie later served as a member of the Judicial Conference of the United States and assumed senior status on the Third Circuit in 1971.

summary: Ron Brown, former chairman of the Democratic National Committee, was confirmed as U.S. Secretary of Commerce in January 1993, becoming the first African American to hold the post. He led major overseas trade missions before his death in a 1996 plane crash.
locationLabel: Washington, D.C., seat of the U.S. Department of Commerce

claims.object: Brown's appointment as Secretary of Commerce was confirmed by the Senate on January 21, 1993, and he took office the next day, the first African American to hold the position.
claims.citationLabel: Miller Center: Ronald Brown (1993-1996)

claims.object: As Commerce Secretary, Brown led trade missions to five continents that generated more than $80 billion in foreign deals for U.S. businesses before his death in a 1996 plane crash in Croatia.
claims.citationLabel: Black Enterprise: The Legacy of Commerce Secretary Ron Brown

historicalContext: Brown had previously chaired the Democratic National Committee from 1987 to 1993, the first African American to lead a major U.S. political party.

summary: Mike Espy, a former Mississippi congressman, was confirmed as U.S. Secretary of Agriculture in January 1993, becoming the first African American and the first Deep South native to hold the post.
locationLabel: Washington, D.C., seat of the U.S. Department of Agriculture

claims.object: Espy was confirmed by the Senate in late January 1993 as Secretary of Agriculture, becoming the first African American and the first Deep South native to hold the position.
claims.citationLabel: Miller Center: Michael Espy (1993-1994)

claims.object: Espy had represented Mississippi's 2nd congressional district in the U.S. House from 1987 to 1993 before resigning to join the Cabinet.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. House of Representatives: Alphonso Michael (Mike) Espy

historicalContext: Espy resigned as Agriculture Secretary in October 1994 amid a gifts investigation and was later acquitted of all charges in 1998.

summary: Alexis Herman, raised in the segregated South, was sworn in May 9, 1997, as U.S. Secretary of Labor, becoming the first African American to hold the post. She mediated the 1997 UPS workers' strike within her first months in office.
locationLabel: Washington, D.C., seat of the U.S. Department of Labor

claims.object: The Senate confirmed Herman as Secretary of Labor on April 30, 1997, and she was sworn in on May 9, 1997, becoming the first African American to hold the position.
claims.citationLabel: NPR: Alexis Herman, the First Black Secretary of Labor in U.S. History, Dies at 77

claims.object: As Labor Secretary, Herman served as an instrumental mediator in the 1997 United Parcel Service workers' strike, helping settle the dispute after fifteen days.
claims.citationLabel: The Hill: Alexis Herman, First Black Labor Secretary, Dies at 77

historicalContext: Herman served as Secretary of Labor from 1997 to 2001, under President Clinton.

summary: William T. Coleman Jr., co-author of the legal brief in Brown v. Board of Education and the Supreme Court's first Black law clerk, was appointed Secretary of Transportation in 1975, becoming only the second African American Cabinet secretary in U.S. history.
locationLabel: Washington, D.C., seat of the U.S. Department of Transportation

claims.object: President Ford appointed Coleman Secretary of Transportation in 1975; he served from March 1975 to January 1977, the second African American Cabinet secretary after Robert C. Weaver.
claims.citationLabel: Transportation History: William T. Coleman, Jr., U.S. Secretary of Transportation

claims.object: In 1948, Coleman became the U.S. Supreme Court's first Black law clerk, and he later co-authored the legal brief for Brown v. Board of Education (1954).
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: William T. Coleman Jr. (1920-2017)

historicalContext: Coleman was awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1995 for a career spanning civil rights litigation and federal service.

summary: Jesse Brown, a disabled Marine Corps veteran of the Vietnam War, was confirmed unanimously as U.S. Secretary of Veterans Affairs in January 1993, becoming the first African American to hold the post and the first former enlisted service member to lead the department.
locationLabel: Washington, D.C., seat of the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs

claims.object: The Senate confirmed Brown unanimously on January 21, 1993, and he was sworn in the next day as Secretary of Veterans Affairs, the first African American to lead the department.
claims.citationLabel: VA News: Remembering Former VA Secretary Jesse Brown

claims.object: Brown was also the first former enlisted member of the U.S. armed forces to be named Secretary of Veterans Affairs, having served in the Marine Corps in Vietnam before a long career at the Disabled American Veterans organization.
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: Jesse Brown (1944-2002)

historicalContext: Brown led the VA from 1993 to 1997, expanding services for female, homeless, and chemically exposed veterans while increasing the department's funding.

summary: Hazel R. O'Leary was confirmed by unanimous consent as U.S. Secretary of Energy in January 1993, becoming the first woman and first African American to hold the post. She later declassified Cold War-era radiation experiment records.
locationLabel: Washington, D.C., seat of the U.S. Department of Energy

claims.object: The Senate confirmed O'Leary by unanimous consent on January 21, 1993, making her the first woman and first African American Secretary of Energy.
claims.citationLabel: Wikipedia: Hazel R. O'Leary

claims.object: As Energy Secretary, O'Leary declassified documents detailing secret Cold War-era radiation testing on unsuspecting American citizens and announced a settlement payment for victims' families.
claims.citationLabel: NC GreenPower: Black History Month, Hazel O'Leary

historicalContext: O'Leary served as Energy Secretary from 1993 to 1997 under President Clinton.

summary: Franklin Raines was named director of the White House Office of Management and Budget in 1996, becoming the first African American to hold the post overseeing the entire federal budget, and helped negotiate the Balanced Budget Act of 1997.
locationLabel: Washington, D.C., seat of the Office of Management and Budget

claims.object: Raines served as Director of the Office of Management and Budget from 1996 to 1998, the first African American to hold the post.
claims.citationLabel: Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget: Franklin Raines

claims.object: As OMB director, Raines helped negotiate the 1997 Balanced Budget Act, contributing to the federal budget surpluses that followed.
claims.citationLabel: The Seattle Times: 'Marginal Man' Finds 'Middle' for Budget Pact

historicalContext: Raines's OMB tenure came amid a wave of Clinton-era Black Cabinet-level appointments across federal agencies.

summary: Rod Paige, former superintendent of the Houston Independent School District, was confirmed January 21, 2001, as U.S. Secretary of Education, becoming the first African American to hold the post and the first former school superintendent to lead the department.
locationLabel: Washington, D.C., seat of the U.S. Department of Education

claims.object: The Senate confirmed Paige as the 7th U.S. Secretary of Education on January 21, 2001, making him the first African American to hold the position.
claims.citationLabel: Miller Center: Rod Paige (2001-2005)

claims.object: Paige was the first former school district superintendent to serve as U.S. Secretary of Education, having led the Houston Independent School District beginning in 1994.
claims.citationLabel: NBC News: Rod Paige, Nation's First African American Secretary of Education, Dies at 92

historicalContext: Paige served as Education Secretary from 2001 to 2005 and was central to developing the No Child Left Behind Act of 2002.

summary: Colin Powell became the youngest and first Black Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff in 1989, serving through the Gulf War, then in 2001 became the first African American U.S. Secretary of State, the highest-ranking Black American in the federal executive branch before Barack Obama's election.
locationLabel: The Pentagon, Arlington, Virginia

claims.object: Powell served as the 12th Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff from 1989 to 1993, the youngest officer and first African American to hold either the chairmanship or the Army's top command position.
claims.citationLabel: CNN: How Benjamin O. Davis Sr. and Davis Jr. Became America's First Black Generals

claims.object: Powell was sworn in as the 65th U.S. Secretary of State on January 20, 2001, becoming the first African American to hold the office.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian: Colin Luther Powell

historicalContext: Powell served as Chairman of the Joint Chiefs at the Pentagon before his later Cabinet post; he remained, with successor Condoleezza Rice, the highest-ranking Black American in the federal executive branch until Barack Obama's 2008 election.

summary: Condoleezza Rice was sworn in January 26, 2005, as the first African American woman to serve as U.S. Secretary of State; she had already become the first woman to serve as National Security Advisor under President George W. Bush.
locationLabel: Washington, D.C., seat of the U.S. Department of State

claims.object: Rice was sworn in as the 66th U.S. Secretary of State on January 26, 2005, becoming the first African American woman to hold the office.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian: Condoleezza Rice

claims.object: Rice had earlier served as National Security Advisor from 2001 to 2005, the first woman to hold that position.
claims.citationLabel: National Women's History Museum: Condoleezza Rice

historicalContext: Rice was the second consecutive Black Secretary of State, following Colin Powell, during the George W. Bush administration.

summary: Eric Holder was confirmed by the Senate on February 2, 2009, and installed the next day as U.S. Attorney General, becoming the first African American to hold the nation's chief law enforcement post.
locationLabel: Washington, D.C., seat of the U.S. Department of Justice

claims.object: The Senate confirmed Holder as Attorney General on February 2, 2009, by a vote of 75 to 21, and he was installed the next day, becoming the first African American to hold the office.
claims.citationLabel: Miller Center: Eric H. Holder (2009-2015)

claims.object: Holder served as Attorney General from 2009 to 2015, the fourth-longest tenure in the office's history.
claims.citationLabel: Black Enterprise: Black History Month, Eric Holder, Attorney General

historicalContext: Holder's tenure spanned the first six years of the Obama administration, including major civil rights and voting rights enforcement initiatives.

summary: Barack Obama was inaugurated January 20, 2009, on the steps of the U.S. Capitol as the 44th President of the United States, becoming the first African American to hold the nation's highest office, before an estimated crowd of 1.8 million people.
locationLabel: United States Capitol, Washington, D.C.

claims.object: Obama was inaugurated as the 44th President of the United States on the steps of the U.S. Capitol on January 20, 2009, becoming the first African American president.
claims.citationLabel: History.com: Barack Obama Is Inaugurated

claims.object: An estimated 1.8 million people attended Obama's inauguration, surpassing the previous record crowd set at Lyndon B. Johnson's 1965 inauguration.
claims.citationLabel: National Geographic Education: Obama Becomes President

historicalContext: Obama took his oath on the Bible used at Abraham Lincoln's first 1861 inauguration, administered by Chief Justice John Roberts.

summary: Kamala Harris was sworn in January 20, 2021, outside the U.S. Capitol as the 49th Vice President of the United States, becoming the first woman, first Black American, and first person of South Asian descent to hold the office.
locationLabel: United States Capitol, Washington, D.C.

claims.object: Harris was sworn in as Vice President on the west front of the U.S. Capitol on January 20, 2021, by Justice Sonia Sotomayor, becoming the first woman, first Black American, and first South Asian American vice president.
claims.citationLabel: History.com: Kamala Harris Becomes First Female Vice President

claims.object: Harris took her oath with her hand on two Bibles, one belonging to a family friend and the other to civil rights icon and Supreme Court Justice Thurgood Marshall.
claims.citationLabel: CNBC: Kamala Devi Harris Is Sworn In as the Country's First Female, Black, Asian American Vice President

historicalContext: Harris was sworn in on the 100th anniversary year of the ratification of women's suffrage in the United States.

summary: Ketanji Brown Jackson, a former federal public defender, was confirmed by the Senate in April 2022 and sworn in June 30, 2022, as the 116th Justice of the U.S. Supreme Court, becoming the first Black woman to serve on the nation's highest court.
locationLabel: Supreme Court of the United States, 1 First Street NE, Washington, D.C.

claims.object: Jackson was sworn in as the 116th Justice of the U.S. Supreme Court on June 30, 2022, becoming the first Black woman to serve on the Court.
claims.citationLabel: NPR: Ketanji Brown Jackson Sworn In as First Black Woman on Supreme Court

claims.object: Jackson was also the first former federal public defender to serve on the U.S. Supreme Court.
claims.citationLabel: SCOTUSblog: Jackson Becomes First Black Woman to Serve on Supreme Court

historicalContext: The Senate confirmed Jackson 53-47 in April 2022; she took her seat when Justice Stephen Breyer's retirement took effect that June.

summary: Loretta Lynch was sworn in April 27, 2015, as U.S. Attorney General, becoming the first African American woman to hold the nation's chief law enforcement post, after one of the longest Senate confirmation processes in history.
locationLabel: Washington, D.C., seat of the U.S. Department of Justice

claims.object: Lynch was sworn in as U.S. Attorney General on April 27, 2015, by then-Vice President Joe Biden, becoming the first African American woman to hold the position.
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopaedia Britannica: Loretta Lynch

claims.object: Lynch's confirmation, which passed the Senate 56-43 on April 23, 2015, took 166 days, one of the longest confirmation processes for an Attorney General in U.S. history.
claims.citationLabel: The Christian Science Monitor: Loretta Lynch Makes History as First Black Woman to Become Attorney General

historicalContext: As Attorney General from 2015 to 2017, Lynch prioritized police reform, opening a civil rights investigation of the Baltimore Police Department in her first weeks in office.

summary: Susan Rice was sworn in as U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations in 2009, becoming the first African American woman to hold the post, before serving as National Security Advisor from 2013 to 2017.
locationLabel: United States Mission to the United Nations, 799 United Nations Plaza, New York, New York

claims.object: Rice served as U.S. Representative to the United Nations from 2009 to 2013, the first African American woman to hold the ambassadorship.
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: Susan Elizabeth Rice (1964- )

claims.object: In 2013, Rice became National Security Advisor, the second African American and second woman to hold that role after Condoleezza Rice.
claims.citationLabel: Obama White House Archives: Ambassador Susan Rice

historicalContext: Rice's UN ambassadorship anchors this record since it was her first singular "first," carried out from the U.S. Mission's headquarters across from UN headquarters in New York.

summary: Retired four-star Army general Lloyd Austin was confirmed by the Senate 93-2 and sworn in January 22, 2021, as U.S. Secretary of Defense, becoming the first African American to lead the Pentagon.
locationLabel: The Pentagon, Arlington, Virginia

claims.object: Austin was confirmed by the Senate 93-2 on January 22, 2021, and sworn in the same day as the 28th U.S. Secretary of Defense, the first African American to hold the post.
claims.citationLabel: Forbes: Lloyd Austin Sworn In as First Black Defense Secretary in U.S. History

claims.object: Austin was ceremonially sworn in a second time on January 25, 2021, by Vice President Kamala Harris.
claims.citationLabel: The American Legion: Lloyd Austin Makes History as First African American Secretary of Defense

historicalContext: Austin served as a four-star Army general before his Cabinet appointment, leading U.S. Central Command from 2013 to 2016.

summary: Henry Ossian Flipper, born enslaved in Georgia, graduated from the United States Military Academy at West Point on June 14, 1877, becoming the first African American to graduate from West Point and the first non-white officer to lead Buffalo Soldiers of the 10th Cavalry.
locationLabel: United States Military Academy, West Point, New York

claims.object: Flipper graduated from the United States Military Academy at West Point on June 14, 1877, fiftieth in a class of seventy-six, becoming the first African American graduate of West Point.
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: The First African American Graduate of West Point

claims.object: Commissioned a second lieutenant and assigned to the 10th U.S. Cavalry at Fort Sill, Flipper became the first non-white officer to lead Buffalo Soldiers in the Regular Army.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. Army: The Legacy of Henry O. Flipper in the U.S. Army

historicalContext: Flipper was dismissed from the Army in 1882 on disputed charges; the Army upgraded his discharge to honorable in 1976, and President Clinton granted him a full pardon in 1999.

summary: Benjamin Oliver Davis Sr. was promoted to brigadier general on October 25, 1940, becoming the U.S. Army's first African American general officer, after rising through the ranks including command of the New York National Guard's 369th Infantry Regiment.
locationLabel: Arlington National Cemetery, Section 2, Arlington, Virginia

claims.object: Davis was promoted to brigadier general on October 25, 1940, becoming the U.S. Army's first African American general officer.
claims.citationLabel: Army Heritage Center Foundation: Defending the Long Road to Freedom, Benjamin Oliver Davis

claims.object: Davis is buried in Section 2 of Arlington National Cemetery, near the grave of his son, Air Force General Benjamin O. Davis Jr.
claims.citationLabel: Find a Grave: GEN Benjamin Oliver Davis Jr. (cross-reference to Sr. burial location)

historicalContext: From 1941 to 1944, Davis Sr. conducted inspection tours of African American Army units and advised on policies affecting Black troop deployment and training.

summary: Benjamin O. Davis Jr., the only Black cadet at West Point during his four years there, commanded the Tuskegee Airmen's 99th Fighter Squadron and 332nd Fighter Group in World War II and became the first Black general in the U.S. Air Force; in 1998 he was advanced to four-star general in retirement.
locationLabel: Arlington National Cemetery, Section 2, Arlington, Virginia

claims.object: Davis Jr. became the first African American general officer in the U.S. Air Force, commanding the Tuskegee Airmen's 332nd Fighter Group in combat over Europe during World War II.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. Air Force: Tuskegee Airman Goes On to Become First Air Force African-American General

claims.object: On December 9, 1998, President Clinton advanced the retired Davis Jr. to four-star general; he is buried in Section 2 of Arlington National Cemetery near his father, General Benjamin O. Davis Sr.
claims.citationLabel: National Museum of the United States Air Force: Benjamin O. Davis Jr.

historicalContext: The Tuskegee Airmen under Davis Jr.'s command flew more than 15,000 sorties and shot down 112 enemy aircraft, one of the most decorated fighter units of the war.

summary: Charles Young, the third African American graduate of West Point, became the U.S. Army's first Black colonel and its highest-ranking Black officer until his death in 1922. He led a cavalry charge against Pancho Villa's forces in 1916 and taught military science at Wilberforce University.
locationLabel: Charles Young Buffalo Soldiers National Monument, 1120 US Route 42, Wilberforce, Ohio

claims.object: Young was promoted to colonel in 1917, becoming the first African American to reach that rank in the U.S. Army and the highest-ranking Black officer in the Regular Army until his death in 1922.
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Brigadier General Charles Young, Charles Young Buffalo Soldiers National Monument

claims.object: This house, where Young lived while a professor of military science at nearby Wilberforce University, was designated the Charles Young Buffalo Soldiers National Monument by President Obama in 2013.
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Charles Young Buffalo Soldiers National Monument

historicalContext: In 1916, Young led a cavalry charge against Pancho Villa's forces in Mexico without losing a single soldier, one of the highlights of a career that also included service as the first Black U.S. national park superintendent.

summary: Doris "Dorie" Miller, a mess attendant aboard the USS West Virginia, carried wounded sailors to safety and manned an anti-aircraft gun during the December 7, 1941 attack on Pearl Harbor. In May 1942 he became the first Black American awarded the Navy Cross.
locationLabel: Waco, Texas, Miller's hometown and site of the Doris Miller Memorial

claims.object: Miller received the Navy Cross from Admiral Chester Nimitz on May 27, 1942, for his actions during the attack on Pearl Harbor, becoming the first African American awarded the Navy Cross.
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopaedia Britannica: Doris Miller

claims.object: Miller's hometown of Waco, Texas, dedicated the Doris Miller Memorial in his honor, commemorating his Pearl Harbor heroism.
claims.citationLabel: City of Waco: Doris Miller Memorial

historicalContext: Miller was killed in action in November 1943 when his escort carrier, USS Liscome Bay, was torpedoed during the Gilbert Islands campaign.

summary: Vernon Baker, an Army lieutenant, led an assault that eliminated multiple enemy positions near Viareggio, Italy in April 1945. Denied recognition for decades due to racial bias in the awards process, he received the Medal of Honor from President Clinton in 1997, one of seven Black World War II recipients.
locationLabel: Arlington National Cemetery, Section 59, Arlington, Virginia

claims.object: In a 1997 White House ceremony, Baker was one of seven African Americans awarded the Medal of Honor for World War II service, and the only one of the seven still living to accept it in person.
claims.citationLabel: The National WWII Museum: First Lieutenant Vernon Baker's Medal of Honor

claims.object: Baker died in 2010 and was buried with full military honors in Section 59 of Arlington National Cemetery.
claims.citationLabel: VA News: Vernon Baker, Recognition Through Adversity

historicalContext: A 1993 Army-commissioned Shaw University study found systematic racial discrimination had denied Black World War II soldiers Medal of Honor consideration, leading to the 1997 awards.

summary: Marcelite Jordan Harris, a Spelman College graduate and Air Force maintenance officer, became the first Black woman general in the U.S. Air Force in 1991 and, in 1995, the first Black woman major general in the history of the U.S. armed forces.
locationLabel: Arlington National Cemetery, Arlington, Virginia

claims.object: In 1991, Harris became the first African American woman brigadier general in the U.S. Air Force; on May 25, 1995, she became the first Black woman major general in the history of the U.S. armed forces.
claims.citationLabel: Texas State Historical Association: Marcelite Cecile Jordan Harris

claims.object: Harris was buried with full military honors alongside her husband at Arlington National Cemetery on February 7, 2019.
claims.citationLabel: NBC News: Marcelite J. Harris, First Black Female Major General, Buried at Arlington

historicalContext: Harris retired in 1997 as the highest-ranking female officer in the Air Force and the nation's highest-ranking African American woman in the Department of Defense.

summary: Michelle Howard, a 1982 U.S. Naval Academy graduate, became the first Black woman to command a Navy combatant ship in 1999 and, in 2014, the first woman four-star admiral in U.S. history, later commanding NATO's Allied Joint Force Command Naples.
locationLabel: United States Naval Academy, Annapolis, Maryland

claims.object: In 1999, Howard became the first Black woman to command a U.S. Navy combatant ship, USS Rushmore.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. Navy: Admiral Michelle Howard

claims.object: Howard was promoted to four-star admiral in 2014, becoming the first woman in U.S. history to hold that rank and the highest-ranking Black woman in Navy history.
claims.citationLabel: Black Enterprise: Michelle Howard Becomes Navy's First Female Four-Star Admiral

historicalContext: Howard graduated from the U.S. Naval Academy in 1982 and went on to command U.S. Naval Forces Europe-Africa and NATO's Allied Joint Force Command Naples from 2016 to 2017.

summary: Roscoe Robinson Jr., a 1951 West Point graduate decorated for combat leadership in Vietnam, became the first African American to command the 82nd Airborne Division in 1976 and, on August 30, 1982, the first Black four-star general in U.S. Army history.
locationLabel: Arlington National Cemetery, Arlington, Virginia

claims.object: On August 30, 1982, Robinson became the first African American to attain the rank of four-star general in the United States Army.
claims.citationLabel: The Army Historical Foundation: Soldier, General Roscoe Robinson Jr.

claims.object: Robinson was also the first African American to command the 82nd Airborne Division, assigned in 1976, and later served as U.S. Military Representative to the NATO Military Committee.
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: Roscoe Robinson, Jr. (1928-1993)

historicalContext: Robinson died in 1993 at Walter Reed Army Medical Center and was buried at Arlington National Cemetery.

summary: Daniel "Chappie" James Jr., a Tuskegee-trained fighter pilot who flew combat missions in Korea and Vietnam, was promoted to four-star general on September 1, 1975, becoming the first African American to reach four-star rank in any branch of the U.S. armed forces.
locationLabel: Arlington National Cemetery, Section 2, Arlington, Virginia

claims.object: James was promoted to four-star general on September 1, 1975, and assigned commander in chief of the North American Air Defense Command, becoming the first African American to reach four-star rank in the U.S. armed forces.
claims.citationLabel: National Museum of the United States Air Force: Gen. Daniel 'Chappie' James Jr.

claims.object: James died in 1978 and was buried with full military honors in Section 2 of Arlington National Cemetery.
claims.citationLabel: The Washington Post: Mondale Heads Mourners at James Burial

historicalContext: James trained Black pilots at Tuskegee during World War II before a combat career spanning the Korean and Vietnam wars.

summary: Samuel L. Gravely Jr. became, in 1961, the first Black naval officer to command a U.S. Navy ship, and in 1971 the first Black officer promoted to flag rank in the Navy, ultimately retiring as a vice admiral after commanding the Third Fleet.
locationLabel: Arlington National Cemetery, Section 66, Arlington, Virginia

claims.object: In 1961, Gravely became temporary commanding officer of the USS Theodore E. Chandler, making him the first African American naval officer to command a U.S. Navy ship.
claims.citationLabel: U.S. Naval Institute: Samuel L. Gravely Jr.

claims.object: Gravely was elevated to Rear Admiral in July 1971, becoming the first African American to reach flag rank in the U.S. Navy, and later commanded the Third Fleet as a vice admiral from 1976 to 1978.
claims.citationLabel: VA News: Veteran of the Day, Navy Veteran Samuel L. Gravely Jr.

historicalContext: Gravely is buried in Section 66 of Arlington National Cemetery following his death in 2004.

summary: Frank E. Petersen Jr., commissioned in 1952, became the first African American Marine Corps aviator, and on February 23, 1979, the first Black general in Marine Corps history, after flying 350 combat missions over two wars.
locationLabel: Arlington National Cemetery, Arlington, Virginia

claims.object: Petersen was commissioned in 1952 and became the Marine Corps' first Black aviator; on February 23, 1979, he was promoted to brigadier general, becoming the first African American general in Marine Corps history.
claims.citationLabel: Smithsonian National Air and Space Museum: The First Black Marine Corps Pilot, Frank E. Petersen Jr.

claims.object: Petersen flew 350 combat missions over the Korean and Vietnam wars and became the first African American to command a fighter squadron, a fighter air group, an air wing, and a major Marine Corps base.
claims.citationLabel: United States Marine Corps: Lieutenant General Frank Petersen Dies at 83

historicalContext: Petersen retired in 1988 after 38 years of service and was buried at Arlington National Cemetery following his death in 2015.

summary: Hazel Winifred Johnson-Brown, who had been denied nursing school admission in her native Pennsylvania because she was Black, became in 1979 the first Black woman general in the U.S. Army and the first Black chief of the Army Nurse Corps, commanding more than 7,000 nurses.
locationLabel: Arlington National Cemetery, Section 60, Arlington, Virginia

claims.object: In 1979, Johnson-Brown was promoted to brigadier general and named chief of the U.S. Army Nurse Corps, becoming the first Black woman general in the United States Army and commanding more than 7,000 nurses.
claims.citationLabel: National Museum of the United States Army: Hazel Johnson-Brown

claims.object: Johnson-Brown died in 2011 and is buried in Section 60 of Arlington National Cemetery.
claims.citationLabel: Find a Grave: Dr. Hazel Winifred Johnson-Brown

historicalContext: Johnson-Brown served in the Army from 1955 to 1983 and went on to earn a doctorate in educational administration from the Catholic University of America.

summary: Charity Adams Earley became the first African American woman commissioned as an officer in the Women's Army Auxiliary Corps in 1942, and in 1945 commanded the 6888th Central Postal Directory Battalion, the only Black WAC unit deployed overseas in World War II. Fort Lee, Virginia was renamed Fort Gregg-Adams in her honor in 2023.
locationLabel: Fort Gregg-Adams (formerly Fort Lee), Prince George County, Virginia

claims.object: Adams was commissioned a second lieutenant on August 29, 1942, becoming the first African American woman officer in the Women's Army Auxiliary Corps, later the WACs.
