claims.citationLabel: The National WWII Museum: Colonel Charity Adams, 6888th Commanding Officer

claims.object: On April 27, 2023, Fort Lee, Virginia was renamed Fort Gregg-Adams in honor of Lieutenant General Arthur J. Gregg and Lieutenant Colonel Charity Adams Earley.
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopedia Virginia: Fort Gregg-Adams (formerly Fort Lee)

historicalContext: In 1945, Adams commanded the 6888th Central Postal Directory Battalion, the only African American and multi-ethnic WAC unit deployed overseas, which cleared a six-month mail backlog in England in three months; she and the battalion received the Congressional Gold Medal in 2022.

summary: Nancy Leftenant-Colon, repeatedly denied entry to the military as a Black nurse, was accepted as an Army Nurse Corps reservist in 1945 and, after President Truman's 1948 desegregation order, became the first Black woman integrated into the Regular Army Nurse Corps.
locationLabel: Amityville, New York, Leftenant-Colon's longtime home

claims.object: Following President Truman's Executive Order 9981 desegregating the military, Leftenant-Colon applied for and received regular Army Nurse Corps status in 1948, becoming the first Black woman integrated into the Regular Army Nurse Corps.
claims.citationLabel: Army Times: Nancy Leftenant-Colon, First Black Woman in Army Nurse Corps, Has Died

claims.object: After graduating from nursing school in 1941 and being repeatedly rejected because of her race, Leftenant-Colon was accepted as an Army Nurse Corps reservist in January 1945.
claims.citationLabel: The Washington Post: Nancy Leftenant-Colon, Barrier-Breaking Military Nurse, Dies at 104

historicalContext: Leftenant-Colon later served as a flight nurse in the Air Force and died in Amityville, New York, in January 2025 at age 104.

displayName: Frederick Douglass (U.S. Marshal and 1872 Vice-Presidential Nominee)
summary: Beyond his abolitionist career, Frederick Douglass was confirmed by the Senate on March 17, 1877, as U.S. Marshal for the District of Columbia, the first African American to hold the post, and in 1872 became the first Black person nominated for U.S. Vice President, on Victoria Woodhull's Equal Rights Party ticket.
locationLabel: Cedar Hill (Frederick Douglass National Historic Site), 1411 W Street SE, Washington, D.C.

claims.object: The Senate confirmed Douglass as U.S. Marshal for the District of Columbia on March 17, 1877, making him the first African American to hold the post.
claims.citationLabel: Boundary Stones (WETA): Frederick Douglass's Career in D.C. Government

claims.object: In 1872, Douglass became the first African American nominated for Vice President of the United States, named without his knowledge as Victoria Woodhull's running mate on the Equal Rights Party ticket.
claims.citationLabel: The Harvard Gazette: 1872 Election, Victoria Woodhull Picks Frederick Douglass as VP

historicalContext: This record is anchored at Cedar Hill, Douglass's Anacostia home from 1877 until his death in 1895, as his primary documented Washington residence during the years of both these government-related firsts; the site itself is catalogued separately as an existing place-kind entry.

summary: Edward R. Dudley, a civil rights lawyer recruited by Thurgood Marshall to the NAACP, was promoted to Ambassador upon the 1949 elevation of the U.S. mission in Liberia, becoming the first African American to hold the rank of U.S. Ambassador.
locationLabel: Roanoke, Virginia, Dudley's hometown

claims.object: In 1949, when the U.S. mission in Liberia was elevated to a full embassy, Dudley was promoted to Ambassador, becoming the first African American to hold the rank of U.S. Ambassador.
claims.citationLabel: National Museum of American Diplomacy: The Legacy of Edward R. Dudley, First African American Ambassador

claims.object: As ambassador, Dudley documented and challenged racial discrimination against Black State Department employees, prompting a change in assignment practices after meeting with Undersecretary of State Dean Acheson.
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopaedia Britannica: Edward R. Dudley

historicalContext: Dudley later served as a justice of the New York State Supreme Court; his diplomatic career is commemorated in his hometown of Roanoke, Virginia.

summary: James Wormley Jones, a Washington, D.C. police detective and decorated World War I infantry captain, was sworn in on November 19, 1919, as the first African American special agent of the Bureau of Investigation, later the FBI.
locationLabel: Washington, D.C., where Jones served in the Bureau of Investigation's General Intelligence Division

claims.object: Jones was appointed the first African American special agent of the Bureau of Investigation on November 19, 1919, by director A. Bruce Bielaski, assigned to its General Intelligence Division.
claims.citationLabel: Federal Bureau of Investigation: Our History, Our Service, 100 Years of African American Special Agents

claims.object: As an undercover agent known internally as Agent 800, Jones's investigations included the case against Marcus Garvey and the African Blood Brotherhood before he left the Bureau in 1923.
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: James Wormley Jones (1884-1958)

historicalContext: Before joining the Bureau, Jones served with the Washington Metropolitan Police Department and commanded a company of the 368th Infantry, 92nd Division, in combat in France during World War I.

summary: Patrick H. Raymond, son of a formerly enslaved abolitionist, was appointed Chief Engineer of the Cambridge, Massachusetts Fire Department on January 5, 1871, becoming the first known Black fire chief in the United States. He commanded the department during the Great Boston Fire of 1872.
locationLabel: Cambridge, Massachusetts, where Raymond served as fire chief

claims.object: Cambridge Mayor Hamlin Harding appointed Raymond Chief Engineer of the Cambridge Fire Department on January 5, 1871, making him the first known Black fire chief in the United States.
claims.citationLabel: City of Cambridge, Massachusetts: Chief Patrick H. Raymond, First Black Fire Chief in the Nation

claims.object: As chief, Raymond commanded the Cambridge Fire Department's response to the Great Fire of Boston in 1872, working alongside Boston Fire Chief Damrell.
claims.citationLabel: The Cambridge Room (Cambridge Public Library): Patrick Henry Raymond, America's First Black Fire Chief

historicalContext: Raymond's father was a formerly enslaved abolitionist and early pastor of Boston's African Meeting House; Raymond served as chief until 1879 and died in Cambridge in 1892.

summary: Lee P. Brown led police departments in Atlanta, Houston, and New York City before becoming, in 1997, the first African American elected mayor of Houston, Texas, serving three terms through 2004.
locationLabel: Houston, Texas, where Brown served as police chief and later mayor

claims.object: In 1997, Brown was elected the first African American mayor of Houston, Texas, and was re-elected twice, serving through 2004.
claims.citationLabel: Watch The Yard: Dr. Lee Brown Was Houston's First Black Mayor

claims.object: In 1982, Brown became the first African American appointed police chief of Houston, developing the department's Neighborhood Oriented Policing program during his tenure through 1990.
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: Lee P. Brown (1937- )

historicalContext: Brown also served as Atlanta's public safety commissioner from 1978 to 1982 and as New York City police commissioner from 1990 to 1992, before leading the White House drug policy office under President Clinton.

summary: Willie L. Williams became Philadelphia's first African American police commissioner in 1988, then in 1992 the first Black chief of the Los Angeles Police Department, leading it through the department's post-Rodney King reform effort.
locationLabel: Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, where Williams began his law enforcement career

claims.object: Williams was appointed Philadelphia's police commissioner in 1988, the first African American to hold the position, serving four years and earning praise for improving police-community relations.
claims.citationLabel: The Philadelphia Inquirer: Willie L. Williams, First African American Top Cop in Both Philadelphia and Los Angeles

claims.object: In 1992, Williams became the first African American chief of the Los Angeles Police Department, taking over after Daryl Gates's resignation following the Rodney King unrest.
claims.citationLabel: National Law Enforcement Officers Memorial Fund: Willie L. Williams

historicalContext: Williams served as LAPD chief until 1997 and later worked as federal security director for Atlanta's Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport.
